chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: CSCE 420
Course Title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
Section: 400
Time: MWF, 9:10am-10:00am
Location: HRBB 124
Credit Hours: 3

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: Fundamental concepts and techniques of intelligent systems; representation and interpretation of knowledge on a computer; search strategies and control; active research areas and applications such as notational systems, natural language understanding, vision systems, planning algorithms, intelligent agents and expert systems.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
• Design and implement intelligent search methods for building complex problem-solving programs.
  o formulate computational problems as search tasks
  o describe how various search algorithms work and explain differences in their space- and time-complexity
  o design heuristics to improve efficiency of search
• Use knowledge representation methods for adding knowledge-based reasoning in programs.
  o encode rules and information in propositional and first-order logic
  o implement inference algorithms to extract deductions from a knowledge base
  o represent and reason about uncertainty using Bayesian probability
• Implement intelligent decision-making methods in a program
  o generate symbolic plans to achieve goals autonomously using planning algorithms
  o use utility theory to make decisions for intelligent agents

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content: Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach, Stuart J. Russell and Peter Norvig.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Online quizzes (10%): Short (10 minutes) quizzes will be provided through Canvas at the end of each topic.
Assignments (40%): You will implement several Al algorithms throughout the course of the semester. Note that not all assignments bare the same weight in the final grade.
Contests (30%): You will implement Al agents for participating in contests. Your Al agent will be evaluated against several of our baseline players and against all other submissions. Overall winners will be assigned extra credit. Note that not all contests bare the same weight in the final grade.
Midterm exam (20%): The midterm exam will evaluate your theoretical knowledge and understanding.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
You are allocated 6 late days for the semester.
A late day extends an assignment deadline by 24 hours. You may use up to 2 late days per assignment. Assignments submitted more than 48 hours late will be worth at most 50%, and no credit will be given for submissions more than 72 hours late. This policy ensures that feedback can be provided in a timely manner. Late days should be used for circumstances that qualify as excused absences according to Texas

A&M University Student Rule 7. Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for details on what constitutes an excused absence, the types of documentation required, and the relevant reporting timelines.
No justification or supporting documentation will be required when using your allocated 6 late days. However, if you require more than 6 late days during the semester, then valid justification and supporting documentation will need to be provided for all late days, including the initial 6.
As a result, it is strongly advised to use late days only when appropriate and to retain documentation that supports your reasons in case additional late days are needed later in the semester.
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
| Week | Topic | Required Reading | Assignment |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Introduction | R&N, Ch. 1, 2 | P0: Tutorial |
| 2 | Uninformed and informed Search | R&N, Ch. 3.1-4, 3.5-6 | P1: Search |
| 3 | Game Trees, Minimax, Expectimax, Utilities | R&N, Ch. 5.2-5 | Contest 1: MA Search |
| 4 | Algorithmic Game Theory | R&N, Ch. 17.5 | P2: Adversarial planning |
| 5 | Markov Decision Processes | R&N, Ch. 17.1-3 | Contest 2: Game theory |
| 6 | Reinforcement learning | R&N, Ch. 21 | P3: Reinforcement learning |
| 7 | Probability & Bayesian networks representation | R&N, Ch. 13.1-5, Ch. 14.1-2,4 | |
| 8 | Decision Networks, Value of Information, Hidden Markov Models | R&N, Ch. 15.2,5 Ch. 16.5-6 | |
| 9 | Particle Filtering and Apps of HMMs, Naive Bayes | R&N, Ch. 15.2,15.6 Ch. 20.1-20.2 | P4: Bayes Nets and HMMs |
| 10 | Supervised learning: Decision Trees, Perceptron | R&N, Ch. 18.3, 18.6 | P5: Machine learning |
| 11 | Supervised learning: Neural Networks | R&N, Ch. 18.8 | Contest 3: capture the flag |
| 12 | Midterm exam | | |
| 13 | Constraint Satisfaction Problems | R&N, Ch. 6.1, 6.2-5 | |
| 14 | Advanced topics | TBD | |
